data.explanation: “early” is the positive degree; comparative is “earlier” and superlative “earliest.”

easy.question: Which is the positive degree of the adverb used in this sentence — “Rama came ___ this morning.”?
He came {earlier} than I expected.
I {ofter} meet him near my house.

data.explanation: “clearly” (manner) modified by degree adverb “very.”
data.statement: She spoke very clearly in class.

easy.question: Pick the adverb of manner.

data.explanation: Adverbs typically modify verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs

easy.question: Adverbs formed from adjectives by adding -ly usually modify:
She writes {well}.

data.explanation: Adverbs ending in -ly form comparatives with “more” (more swiftly).

easy.question: Which is the correct comparative form of “swiftly”?

data.explanation: “always” expresses frequency and modifies “does.”
data.statement: He always does his homework.

easy.question: Pick the adverb of frequency.

data.explanation: “swiftly” (adverb of manner) modifies the verb “flies.”
data.statement: The crow flies swiftly.

easy.question: Pick the adverb and the word it modifies.

data.explanation: “rather” modifies the adjective “lazy.”

easy.question: Which sentence shows an adverb modifying an adjective?

data.explanation: “badly” → comparative “worse”, superlative “worst”

easy.question: Which of these adverbs is irregular in its comparison?

data.explanation: “creditably” modifies the verb phrase “performed his duty.”
data.statement: He performed his duty creditably.

easy.question: Pick the adverb and its type.
Birds fly {swiftly}.

data.explanation: “rather” (degree adverb) modifies the adjective “lazy.”
data.statement: Rama is a rather lazy boy.

easy.question: Pick the adverb and the adjective it modifies.
He has {often} told me to be careful.

data.explanation: Frequency adverbs (always, often, never) are usually

easy.question: Where is an adverb of frequency usually placed?
He is {always} cheerful.

data.explanation: “keenly” (manner) follows the object “insult” with transitive verb.
data.statement: I feel this insult keenly.

easy.question: Pick the adverb and its direct object (if any).

data.explanation: “enough” follows the word it modifies (e.g., “He was rash enough…”).
options: At the start of sentence always

easy.question: The adverb “enough” is normally placed:
She did the work {carefully}.

data.explanation: “only” before “he” restricts to “he and nobody else.”
data.statement: Only he lent me a book.

easy.question: Pick the adverb that is misplaced if you want to mean “only he and nobody else.”
He spoke {quietly} to the class.

data.explanation: For -ly adverbs superlative is formed with “most” (most neatly).

easy.question: Which is a correct superlative form?
You know well {enough} what I mean.

data.explanation: “late” modifies the intransitive verb “arrived.”
data.statement: They arrived late.

easy.question: Pick the adverb and the verb it modifies.
I shall {certainly} miss him.

data.explanation: “very” is a degree adverb modifying the adjective “happy.”
data.statement: She is very happy today.

easy.question: Pick the adverb that expresses degree.

data.explanation: “often” is an adverb of frequency modifying “thought.”

easy.question: “I have often thought of marrying.” — Which word is the adverb?

data.explanation: Placement of “only” changes meaning; each position is grammatical but changes focus/meaning.
options: Only he lent me a book.
He only lent me a book.
He lent only me a book.
All of the above (each means different thing)

easy.question: Which sentence correctly places “only”?
We {never} see them now.

data.explanation: Placement immediately before intended word avoids ambiguity — “I did only two sums” (not more).

hard.question: Which sentence demonstrates why “only” should be placed immediately before the word it modifies?
I praise him {only when} he deserves it.

data.explanation: “almost” modifies the prepositional phrase “to the top” indicating degree/extent.
data.statement: He had got almost to the top when the rope broke.

hard.question: Pick the adverb(s) and the word(s) they modify.

data.explanation: “often” placed between auxiliary “have” and principal “told.”
data.statement: I have often told him to write neatly.

hard.question: Pick the adverb pair showing correct placement with auxiliary + principal verb.
We {seldom or never} see those forsaken who trust in God.

data.explanation: “only” before “me” restricts recipient to me alone.
options: He merely lent it, didn't give it away.
He lent a book to me and nobody else.
He was the only one who lent a book.
He lent just books, no other things.

hard.question: Which of the following is a correct explanation of the sentence meaning change? “He lent only me a book.”

data.explanation: “most” (degree) immediately precedes the adverb “cruelly.”
data.statement: He struck the horse most cruelly.

hard.question: Pick the adverb that should be placed immediately before the adjective.

data.explanation: Standard placement for manner adverb with infinitive is after verb or at sentence end; A is natural.
options: The servant promised quickly to do it.
Quickly the servant promised to do it.
The servant promised to do it quickly.
The servant promised to quickly do it.

hard.question: Which sentence is correct if you want to express that the servant promised speed but not necessarily the manner?
He has slept {only three} hours.

data.explanation: “quite” (degree) modifies the adverb “angrily.”

hard.question: In “He spoke quite angrily,” “quite” modifies:

data.explanation: “Certainly” modifies the whole sentence/clause (sentence adverb).
data.statement: Certainly, you are wrong.

hard.question: Pick the adverb and show it modifies a clause/sentence.
He {utterly} failed to complete the task.

data.explanation: Both “I haven’t got any” (negation with any) and “I have got none” (affirmative with none) are acceptable; avoid “haven’t… none.”
options: I haven’t got any of the papers.
I haven’t got any of the papers.
I haven’t got any of the papers.
Both A and B are acceptable depending on emphasis.

hard.question: Which is the best correction for a sentence using a double negative? “I haven’t got none of the papers.”

data.explanation: Placement of “ever” is awkward; “we remember ever” is preferable
data.statement: Quite the most remarkable article we ever remember to have read.

hard.question: Pick the misused adverb in the sentence.
Do not use {quite} in the sense of “very” or “to a considerable degree.”

data.explanation: An adverb that modifies an entire sentence is often placed first for emphasis (e.g., Fortunately).

hard.question: Which sentence shows correct placement of an adverb modifying the whole sentence?

data.explanation: Placement of "only" is awkward and changes the intended meaning; "found only" is preferable because the adverb should immediately precede the phrase it actually modifies ("three mistakes," not the verb "found").
data.statement: He only found three mistakes in the entire document.

hard.question: Pick the misused adverb in the sentence.
The adverb {too} means “more than enough.”

data.explanation: “too” means more than enough; should not be used as synonym for “very.”
options: Use “too” to mean “more than enough” and not as replacement for “very” or “much.”

hard.question: Which of these adverb usages is recommended on page 168 about “too” and “very”?

data.explanation: “only” before “a book” restricts what was lent (a book and nothing more).
data.statement: He lent me only a book.

hard.question: Pick the adverb and clause when “only” modifies the object not the verb.
I did {only two sums}. [Not, I only did two sums.]

data.explanation: When a verb is transitive with an object following, the adverb often follows the object
options: Adverb must be at sentence end.

hard.question: Which is correct about adverb placement with a transitive verb and object?

data.explanation: “enough” follows the word (adjective) it modifies, exception to immediate-before rule.
data.statement: This is a house large enough for our purpose.

hard.question: Pick the adverb and show its rule about “enough.”
She read {only three pages}.

data.explanation: “Seldom or never” is correct; “seldom or ever” is incorrect.
options: We seldom or never see those forsaken.
We seldom or ever see those forsaken.
We seldom or never not see those forsaken.
We seldom or ever not see those forsaken.

hard.question: Which sentence demonstrates a correct use of “ever/never” per the notes?
The adverb {ever} is sometimes misused for never.

data.explanation: “Only” before “he” restricts subject to him alone.
data.statement: Only he lent me a book.

hard.question: Pick the adverb and the correct placement to convey “he and nobody else.”

data.explanation: “only” before the clause “when he deserves it” limits praise to those circumstances.
options: Only I praise him when he deserves it.
I only praise him when he deserves it.
I praise him only when he deserves it.
I praise only him when he deserves it.

hard.question: Which placement of “only” would mean “I praise him in those circumstances and not otherwise”?

data.explanation: “quite” (degree) modifies the adverb “angrily.”
data.statement: He spoke quite angrily.

hard.question: Pick the adverb and its role when it modifies another adverb.
Speak {loud enough} to be heard.

medium.question: He speaks English {fluently}.

data.explanation: “frequently” is frequency adverb modifying “visited.”
data.statement: I have frequently visited him while in Delhi.

medium.question: Pick the adverb pair (adverb, verb/adjective) in this sentence.
I did {not} know his address.

data.explanation: Certain adverbs (now, then, here, once, twice, very in some contexts) are not comparable

medium.question: Which of the following adverbs cannot be compared?

data.explanation: “only” modifies “three” (number), meaning exactly three hours.
data.statement: He has slept only three hours.

medium.question: Pick the adverb and the word it modifies.
We {seldom} see him now.

data.explanation: “very” modifies the adjective “heavy.”
data.statement: The load is very heavy for a horse.

medium.question: Pick the adverb of degree and the adjective.

data.explanation: “almost” modifies the verb phrase indicating extent — “had got almost to the top.”

medium.question: In “He had got almost to the top when the rope broke,” “almost” modifies:

data.explanation: Adverbs modifying adjectives/adverbs usually come immediately before them
options: They come immediately before the word they modify.

medium.question: Which is true about adverbs modifying adjectives or other adverbs?

data.explanation: “Fortunately” modifies whole sentence/clause.
data.statement: Fortunately, he was not hurt.

medium.question: Pick the adverb and the clause it modifies.

data.explanation: Frequency adverbs are placed after “to be” (e.g., “He is always cheerful.”).
options: At the start of sentence only

medium.question: Which is the correct placement of frequency adverbs with “to be”?

data.explanation: “not” is placed between auxiliary “did” and principal “know” (negation).
data.statement: He did not know his address.

medium.question: Pick the adverb and the verb phrase it modifies.
He was {rash enough} to interrupt.

data.explanation: “ever” is often misplaced; correct phrase would be “we remember ever…”
options: Quite the most remarkable article we ever remember to have read.

medium.question: Which sentence shows a misplaced “ever” that changes meaning?

data.explanation: “He only lent me a book” implies he merely lent it (didn’t give it away).
options: He only lent me a book.
Only he lent me a book.
He lent only me a book.
He lent me only a book.

medium.question: Which placement of “only” means “he merely lent it, didn’t give it away”?

data.explanation: With a transitive verb, adverb can follow the object (keenly follows insult).
data.statement: I feel this insult keenly.

medium.question: Pick the adverb and the object it follows.
Say it {again} more slowly.

data.explanation: “nearly” is degree adverb modifying the verb “died.”
data.statement: He nearly died of laughing.

medium.question: Pick the adverb and its grammatical role.

data.explanation: “too” (more than enough) modifies the adjective “young.”
data.statement: The child is too young for school.

medium.question: Pick the adverb and the adjective it modifies.

data.explanation: With auxiliary + principal verb, adverb (negation) usually goes between them (did not know).

medium.question: Which is correct: “He did not know his address” — which adverb placement shows negation?
He {utterly} failed in his attempt.

data.explanation: Two negatives like “haven’t… none” destroy each other and are incorrect in standard English.
options: I could not find it anywhere.

medium.question: Which is an example of double negative to avoid?
She returned {immediately} after lunch.

data.explanation: “almost” modifies the prepositional phrase showing extent.
data.statement: He had got almost to the top.

medium.question: Pick the adverb and the prepositional phrase it modifies.
I did only {two} sums.

data.explanation: “enough” follows and modifies “loud” (the word it qualifies).

medium.question: “Speak loud enough to be heard.” The adverb “enough” modifies:
We {never} referred to the matter.

data.explanation: “seldom” is an adverb of frequency modifying “make.”
data.statement: They seldom make a mistake.

medium.question: Pick the adverb and the type.

data.explanation: Adverb usually placed between auxiliary and principal verb (e.g., “I have often told him…”).

medium.question: Which is correct placement when an adverb qualifies an auxiliary and principal verb?
He was {nearly} at the top when the rope broke.
